beam.label: plain_baseline
beam.text: Peter, I'm Beckett: 17 years old, starting Princeton in the fall; building Aurelius (runaurelius.com): constraint-aware orchestration for AI infrastructure. The consensus says AI infrastructure needs more power. I think it needs better planning. Replaying ~1.5M public production requests, my scheduler beat the production one by 724% on SLA-safe goodput per dollar. May I send you the one-page memo? I'm skipping Princeton to build something that actually works.

beam.label: crossover
beam.text:
Peter,

I'm Beckett, 17, starting Princeton in the fall. I'm building Aurelius (runaurelius.com) on a contrarian thesis: AI infrastructure has a planning problem disguised as a power problem. Schedulers optimize the next placement, but nothing chooses the fleet's best trajectory over time. In simulated replay of ~1.5M public production requests, my scheduler beat the production one by 724% on SLA-safe goodput per dollar and cut GPU-hours by 84%. You think about systems that compound over time rather than optimize the local step. I'm skipping Princeton. May I send you the one-page memo?

— Beckett

beam.label: seed_0
beam.text: Mr. Thiel, I'm Beckett, 17, building Aurelius (runaurelius.com): constraint-aware orchestration for AI infrastructure. The thesis is that AI infrastructure has a planning problem disguised as a power problem — schedulers optimize the next placement, but nothing chooses the fleet's best trajectory over time. In simulated replay of ~1.5M requests from public production traces, Aurelius achieved +724% average SLA-safe goodput per dollar vs the production scheduler. You specifically think about systems that compound over time rather than optimize for the local step. May I send you the one-page technical memo? — Beckett

fragility.note: a winner that leads under only one inbox-reality hypothesis is FRAGILE; within_noise arms are indistinguishable at this draw count — the system reports 'best-supported among tested', never 'best possible'.

missing: identity: a stranger cannot tell who is writing from the first two sentences ('I'm <name>, building <thing>')
thesis: no declarative claim beyond the introduction
next_step: no explicit, trivially answerable ask — the recipient cannot tell what replying accomplishes
flags: diligence_bait: the ask requests unpaid technical diligence / debate ('which assumption is wrong?') — high effort, adversarial from a stranger
relevance: no explicit bridge to the recipient (why THEM)

label: model_based_judgment — same UNCALIBRATED persona evaluator for every candidate; 'best-supported among tested', never a theoretical optimum
